aliases: chicken burger meal with french fries

english: chicken burger meal with french fries

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 covers hamburger as a food product; closest available match for a chicken burger. The fries component is not separately coded here; the record represents the combined set meal.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken burger with French fries — composite fast-food meal; FoodEx2 A0FKP (Composite food — mixed dish) used as the closest defensible category for a set meal combining a chicken burger and deep-fried potato fries.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for a chicken burger with fries set meal. A0FKP (composite mixed dish) is the closest defensible anchor. Fries sub-component would map to A0CKL (fried potato products) if coded separately.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chicken burger with fries as a composite set meal.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 320 g, comprising a chicken burger (approximately 180–200 g) and a regular portion of fries (approximately 120–140 g), as commonly served in Singapore fast-food and casual dining outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken burger with fries — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F08020013 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and provenance 'canonical'. All per_100g core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this source. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, and micronutrients not reported by HPB for this entry are set to -1 where not available, except saturated fat which is estimated from analogous HPB fast-food entries and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a chicken burger with fries as a composite meal in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated as a weighted composite: white wheat burger bun (GI ~70–75, University of Sydney reference food) and deep-fried potato chips/fries (GI ~63–75, University of Sydney GI Database entry for french fries). A blended GI of approximately 65 is defensible given the carbohydrate split between bun (~55%) and fries (~45%) in a typical serving. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI 65 × available carbohydrate per serving (85.4 g for 320 g serving at 26.55 g/100g) / 100 = 55.5 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 544 mg/100 g; a 320 g serving delivers approximately 1,741 mg sodium, exceeding the entire recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this meal; if consumed, request no added salt on fries, minimal sauce on the burger, and pair with a low-sodium beverage to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load (~55.5 per serving) driven by refined wheat bun and deep-fried high-GI potato fries, combined with trans fat (0.3 g/100 g) that worsens insulin resistance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this meal due to its high glycaemic load and trans fat content; if a burger is desired, substitute fries with a side salad or corn cup, request a wholegrain bun, and choose a grilled rather than fried chicken patty.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined burden of trans fat (0.3 g/100 g), saturated fat (~3.8 g/100 g), and sodium (544 mg/100 g) presents a triple cardiovascular risk; a full serving contributes approximately 0.96 g trans fat, which is clinically significant.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this meal; the trans fat and saturated fat content raise LDL cholesterol and lower HDL cholesterol, while the high sodium load elevates blood pressure — all established independent risk factors for adverse cardiovascular events.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (0.3 g/100 g) and deep-frying-generated advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) from fries are strongly pro-inflammatory and directly worsen joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this meal entirely; if a burger is desired, request a grilled chicken patty with no fries and verify the bun contains zero trans fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Burger with Fries is a Western-style fast-food set meal consisting of a chicken patty (typically breaded and deep-fried, or grilled) served in a soft white wheat bun with lettuce, tomato, and mayonnaise or sauce, accompanied by a regular portion of deep-fried potato fries. It is one of the most commonly consumed fast-food meals in Singapore and is widely available at international fast-food chains, local fast-food outlets, and casual dining restaurants. The texture profile combines the soft, slightly chewy bun with the crispy, savoury chicken patty and the crunchy, salted fries. Per 100 g, the meal provides 276 kcal, 9.0 g protein, 14.9 g fat, and 26.6 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 544 mg — placing it firmly in the high-sodium category. A typical 320 g serving delivers approximately 883 kcal and 1,741 mg sodium, which exceeds the full daily sodium recommendation for hypertensive individuals. The meal has a medium-to-high glycaemic index (estimated GI ~65) and a high glycaemic load (~55.5 per serving), making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Trans fat from deep-frying (estimated 0.3 g/100 g) and saturated fat (~3.8 g/100 g) contribute to cardiovascular and inflammatory risk. This meal is classified as halal and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. It is commonly consumed as a working lunch or casual meal. Clinically, this meal is rated high concern for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis.

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry F08020013 with input confidence 0.95 and provenance 'canonical'. Saturated fat and trans fat are estimated from analogous HPB fast-food entries and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database reference foods (wheat bun and french fries) with estimation basis documented. Overall confidence is high for the HPB-sourced macros; GI is an informed estimate.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.8 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.3 g/100 g) are not directly reported in the HPB F08020013 entry but are estimated from analogous HPB fast-food burger and fries entries and from the health_suitability notes in the input record, which explicitly cite these values. These should be verified against the full HPB nutrient table when detailed fatty acid data becomes available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient, fatty acid breakdown, and moisture data for this composite set meal are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F08020013 as of 2026. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database reference values for white wheat bread/bun (GI ~70–75) and french fries (GI ~63–75), weighted by approximate carbohydrate contribution of each component. This is not a directly measured GI for this specific product. 4. Serving size of 320 g is an estimate based on typical Singapore fast-food portion sizes (burger ~180–200 g + fries ~120–140 g); no standardised portion weight is reported in the HPB source for this entry. 5. The input record listed gi_value as 0 with confidence 0.42 from 'Embedded GI Database' — this has been replaced with a defensible analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database per Project Veritas source policy. 6. This is a Western-style international fast-food dish; multilingual local-name fields (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable as this dish does not have established local-language names in Singapore — it is universally referred to in English.
